ai-alvinn-training.text: Describe how ALVINN, the first self-driving car, was trained as an example of supervised learning following three phases: record human-labeled data, learn a function, apply it to new inputs.

ai-bag-of-words.text: Define a bag of words as a model that represents text as an unordered collection of words with frequencies.

ai-centroids.text: Compute a centroid to represent the 'middle' of a labeled group of documents, and use it to search by concept/tag and to make recommendations from a collection of liked/disliked items.

ai-confusion-matrix.text: Read and construct a confusion matrix to evaluate a classifier's accuracy per category, understanding which specific mistakes it makes rather than just an overall score.

ai-create-tree.text: Define a classifier and a decision tree (root node, decision nodes, leaf nodes, paths), and explain how a tree narrows inputs down to a predicted category.

ai-data-normalization.text: Explain the importance of data normalization (removing punctuation, lowercasing, removing stopwords) for cutting out noise and producing meaningful similarity scores

ai-define-nlp.text: Define natural language processing as using computers to process languages, often by consuming a corpus and constructing a model, which can be used to produce new text.

ai-different-kinds.text: Recognize that modern AI is driven by Machine Learning, and classify everyday AI tools into various categories: Prediction (classifiers and regression), Similarity (recommendation), and Generation (language models).

ai-effect.text: Describe the AI effect, the phenomenon where humans continuously redefine intelligence to exclude AI achievements.

ai-hallucination.text: Connect a model's confident-but-wrong predictions on inputs outside its training range to the concept of AI 'hallucination'.

ai-high-dimensions-regression.text: Extend single-variable regression to multiple regression, understanding that a line of best fit in 2D becomes a plane of best fit in 3D, with one slope (weight) per input variable.

ai-high-dimensions-similarity.text: Understand that the distance and angle formulas generalize to any number of dimensions, allowing similarity to be computed in high-dimensional 'spaces'.

ai-machines-v-people.text: Understand that machines and people often use starkly different processes to complete similar tasks, based on the strengths and limitations of computers and human brains.

ai-math-connection-regression.text: Connect the math vocabulary (variable, slope) to machine-learning vocabulary (parameter, weight), and recognize multiple regression as the engine behind real AI systems.

ai-model.text: Define a model as a simplified representation of the relationship(s) between variables in a dataset.

ai-nlp-artificial.text: Recognize that natural language processing, originally developed for use with natural languages, can also be applied to artificial languages.

ai-over-fitting.text: Identify and explain over-fitting — where a model memorizes noise in the training data (especially from highly variable quantitative columns) and fails on new testing data.

ai-plagiarism-detector-ethics.text: Describe how similarity detection powers plagiarism checkers (training corpus → model), and evaluate when using these tools is or isn't ethical or appropriate.

ai-reinforcement-learning.text: Define reinforcement learning as a type of machine learning where the machine agent learns how to maximize its performance through trial and error.

ai-similarity.text: Compare three measures of similarity — simple (exact-match), distance (Euclidean), and angle similarity — and explain the trade-offs of each, including why angle similarity better captures proportion and orientation.

ai-slm.text: Explain how statistical language models use probability — the fraction of times one word follows another in a training corpus — to predict and generate text.

ai-slm-create.text: Construct a language model by decomposing a corpus into n-grams (unigrams, bigrams, trigrams) and computing next-word probabilities, recognizing that a unigram table is effectively a bag of words.

ai-slm-hallucination.text: Sample from a model to generate text, and explain why language models 'make stuff up' and reflect biases in their corpus — they know probability, not meaning or truth.

ai-slm-temperature.text: Explain how temperature controls randomness in word selection and why some randomness is desirable, and connect this to AI 'hallucinations' (where all output is generated by the same process, whether true or not).

ai-supervised-v-unsupervised.text: Distinguish unsupervised learning (finding patterns in unlabeled data) from supervised learning (learning from human-labeled data with an 'answer key').

ai-supervision.text: Explain that supervision is the process of humans providing information on known inputs that the machine learning algorithm can use to extrapolate and make predictions for unknown inputs.

ai-supervision-ethics.text: Recognize the hidden human labor (CAPTCHAs, paid contractors, content moderation) and ethical costs behind the labeling that powers supervised learning.

ai-training-lossy.text: Define training as the act of transforming data from a corpus into a model, usually after aggregating the data in some way and losing some of the original detail of the training data.

ai-tree-algorithm.text: Evaluate why some splits are better than others, choosing questions that divide the data into the 'neatest, most organized piles' possible.

attributes-as-dimensions.text: Almost any category can be described by numeric attributes. Examples in the category can be graphed as points in space defined by those attributes.

data-driven-algos.text: Define data-driven algorithms as algorithms that produce better results when given more representative data.
